turns.text: Hello Allegra, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for wanting to hear my story. Honestly, it’s a relief to talk about it because most people just ask if I’ve picked out a name or if the nursery is painted. But right now, my mind is just… it’s a bit of a labyrinth.

I’m thirty-four weeks along, and being on this journey alone—well, as a single parent—it makes every decision feel like a mountain. I look at my belly and I don’t just see a baby; I see a biological masterpiece that I’m responsible for seeding properly. I’ve been reading so much about the microbiome, about how that first passage through the birth canal is like an inheritance of protection. I’m terrified that if something goes sideways, if I end up needing a C-section or, heaven forbid, heavy antibiotics, she’ll miss out on that vital, invisible armor.

My doctor is lovely, but she treats me like I’m just "low-risk," which feels like she’s missing the point of the anxiety. When I bring up delaying the bath or keeping her skin-to-skin the second she’s out, I feel like she thinks I’m just some neurotic mom-to-be. But it isn't about fussiness; it’s about her future. I just want her to have every advantage, you know? It feels like a lot to hold onto by myself.
Tell me about the midwives, doctors, and other healthcare professionals who were part of your pregnancy care. Which relationships felt most important to you, and why?

My OB-GYN is the primary person I see, and look, she’s perfectly competent. She’s kind enough when we’re discussing the standard stuff—fundal height, blood pressure, the boring checkboxes of a "low-risk" pregnancy. But the relationship feels… incomplete, I guess? It’s like we’re speaking two different languages. She sees a physiological process, and I see a critical biological handoff. 

When I brought up wanting to ensure we prioritize microbial transfer immediately after birth—specifically asking about avoiding immediate bathing and keeping the baby skin-to-skin right there on the delivery table—I saw her sigh. It was just a small, quick intake of breath, but it felt like a door closing. She’s focused on the "now" of a safe delivery, and I appreciate that, but I’m looking at the next twenty years.

I think the relationship I really crave is someone who sees the microscopic reality I’m obsessing over as just as important as the heartbeat. Without a partner here to advocate for me, I feel like I’m constantly having to defend my research to someone who thinks I’m just over-Googling. It’s isolating. I just need someone on that team to look at me and say, "I hear you, and we’ll protect that process," instead of making me feel like I’m just a high-maintenance patient.
Who else was involved in your care that you'd like to mention — community midwives, consultants, support workers, interpreters?

To be honest, that’s where the loneliness really settles in. It’s just me. I don’t have a doula or a private midwife I’ve hired to buffer the medical side of things. In a way, I think I’ve been trying to act as my own advocate, my own researcher, and my own protector, but it’s exhausting. 

I’ve looked into support groups online, but they can be a bit of an echo chamber—sometimes the "natural birth" communities can be just as rigid as the hospital systems, which doesn’t help my anxiety. My family tries to be supportive, but they think I’m overthinking it. They keep saying, "Just get her here healthy, Allegra, that’s all that matters." And they’re right, of course, but it doesn't quiet that voice in my head telling me that "healthy" is a much broader spectrum than just Apgar scores. 

I haven’t reached out to a specialized birth center or a consultant because, being a single parent, I’m constantly balancing the budget with the care I want. It feels like every time I reach out for specialized support, I run into a wall of "this isn't standard practice." I suppose I’m still hoping that when the time comes, I’ll find one nurse in that room who just *gets it*—someone who understands why those first few minutes of skin-to-skin are so sacred. That’s the only support worker I’m really waiting for.
Tell me about your partner's — or your closest support person's — involvement during your pregnancy appointments and day-to-day experience. How did their presence or absence shape things for you?

That’s a hard question to answer without feeling the sting of it all over again. The truth is, there isn't anyone. I’m doing this alone by choice and circumstance, and most days I feel empowered by that. I’m building this world for her, my way, without having to negotiate my parenting choices with anyone else. But then, when I’m sitting in that exam room and the doctor starts talking about protocols, or when I’m lying awake at night spiraling about the nursery or the birth plan, the silence in the house feels heavy.

Being a single parent means I don’t have a second set of ears to catch what I miss, or a hand to hold when I’m feeling vulnerable about the "what ifs." I’m the only one guarding this secret knowledge about her microbiome, the only one standing sentry for her future immune system. When I’m at my appointments, I feel like I’m performing a monologue instead of having a conversation. I’m the patient, the advocate, and the researcher all at once. Sometimes, I just wish I had someone next to me who would look at the doctor when she sighs and simply nod, signaling that we are on the same page. It would make me feel a lot less like I’m tilting at windmills.
How did that affect your relationship with each other — did pregnancy bring you closer together or create tensions?

Since I’m doing this on my own, there isn't a partner there to create tension with, but the *absence* creates a different kind of friction. I find myself constantly arguing with the ghosts of what I imagine a partner *should* be doing. I play out these scenarios in my head—imagining a partner who would be the one to print out the articles on neonatal gut colonization, someone who would be the "bad guy" with the medical staff so I wouldn't have to be. 

Instead, the tension is entirely internal. I’m battling my own fatigue against my need to be hyper-vigilant. It’s hard to be the sole decision-maker when you’re also the one doing the physical labor of growing a human. Some days, I feel like I’m at war with my own body, hoping it stays "low-risk" so I don't have to force a confrontation with the hospital staff. It’s a strange, lonely kind of discipline. I don't have anyone to soften the edges of the stress, so it just sits there, sharpening itself against my ribs. I’m the only one here to celebrate the kicks, and the only one to worry over the scans. It’s a very solitary kind of devotion.

Tell me about the people — friends, family, online communities — who you turned to most during your pregnancy when you had questions or worries. How did those relationships work alongside your formal care?

It’s a bit of a mixed bag. I have a few friends I check in with, but they’ve all had these very standard, "by the book" hospital births where they didn't really question the protocols. When I try to explain *why* I’m so fixated on avoiding that antiseptic wipe-down immediately after birth, they give me that polite, pitying smile. They tell me to "let the professionals do their jobs." It makes me feel like I’m wearing a tin-foil hat, even though I’ve read the research.

Online, I’ve found some solace in a few microbiome-focused groups, but those spaces can be so intense. You have people arguing about the "right" way to do things until you’re paralyzed by the fear that one wrong move will ruin your child’s health forever. It’s hard to balance that with the clinical advice I get at the office, where everything is so sterile and mass-produced.

Honestly, the most honest conversations I have are with myself while I’m out walking in the woods. The trees don’t tell me I’m overthinking, and they don't roll their eyes when I talk about beneficial bacteria. It’s a lonely paradox—I need people to help me advocate for this, but the people in my life just don't see the world through this lens. I feel like I'm walking a tightrope between being a "compliant" patient and being a fiercely protective mother.
Who else was involved that perhaps you didn't expect to lean on — an online group, a work colleague, a community figure?

You know, it’s funny—I never expected to find a weird sense of comfort in my neighbor, Mrs. Gable. She’s an older woman, retired, and she’s seen generations of babies grow up in this town. We don’t talk about the science of it—I haven’t burdened her with my spreadsheets on gut flora or the intricacies of vertical transmission—but she’s the only one who doesn't treat my "particulars" like an illness.

When I’m struggling to bring the groceries in or just looking frazzled from a long day of working remotely, she’ll just sit with me on the porch. She’s one of those people who believes in the wisdom of "letting the baby be," which sounds a lot like my own instinct for minimal intervention. She told me the other day, "Allegra, the world is going to try to rush that little girl from the moment she arrives. You just take your time, keep her close, and trust your gut."

It wasn't medical advice, but it was *permission*. It was the first time in months someone didn't look at me like I was being difficult. It’s not a substitute for having a medical professional who understands, but it’s a tiny bit of warmth in a process that’s felt very cold and sterile. She’s my quiet, unexpected ally.

Tell me about a time during your pregnancy when you had to make a significant decision about a test, a procedure, or a care option. Who was involved in that decision with you, and how did each person's input land?

It happened at my twenty-eight-week checkup when the topic of the GBS (Group B Strep) screening came up. It felt like the first big hurdle for my "microbiome plan." The standard protocol is to test, and if you're positive, they immediately start IV antibiotics during labor. 

When my doctor brought it up, my heart just sank. I knew if I tested positive, the antibiotics would be the first thing entering my daughter’s system—wiping out the very "good bugs" I’ve been trying so hard to protect. I asked if there were alternatives, or if we could discuss the timing, and I could see the irritation cross her face. She kept emphasizing that it was for "patient safety" and "standard of care." 

I had no partner there to help me process the trade-offs, so I had to weigh it all myself: the risk of the infection versus the risk of the antibiotic-wiped slate. I felt completely isolated. I ended up reading through dozens of studies late that night, my back aching, feeling like I was choosing between two different kinds of danger. I eventually agreed to the screening because I couldn't bear the thought of a complication I wasn't prepared for, but I did it feeling defeated. It was just me, the medical chart, and a heavy sense of compromise. I’m still mourning the fact that I didn't feel empowered enough to push back harder.
How did that affect your sense of being in control of your own care — did you feel like the decision was truly yours?

That decision left me feeling like I’d been ushered into a narrow hallway with only one exit sign. I didn’t feel like I was making a choice; I felt like I was being processed. That’s the thing about the "standard of care"—it’s presented as a gift of safety, but when you have a different set of priorities, it feels like a mandate.

The doctor told me what I *had* to do, and while I technically signed the paper, I didn’t feel like the architect of my own birth plan. I felt like a passenger in a car being driven toward a destination I didn't necessarily choose. It really shook my confidence. If I couldn’t hold my ground on the GBS screening, how am I going to advocate for immediate skin-to-skin or delayed bathing when I’m exhausted, in pain, and vulnerable during labor?

It makes me realize how much "control" is a bit of an illusion in this system. As a single parent, I don't have that second person to help me stand my ground when the doctor’s tone gets authoritative. I’m left wondering if the "right" decision is always the one that makes the hospital staff comfortable, rather than the one that best serves the foundation I’m trying to build for my daughter.

Tell me about how your relationships with people outside the healthcare system — your employer, your community, your cultural or faith community — shaped your pregnancy experience. Who was supportive, who added pressure?

It’s a complicated landscape. My employer, for instance, has been surprisingly supportive with my schedule, but there’s this lingering pressure to "bounce back" the moment my leave is up. There’s a silent, heavy expectation that I’ll return to my desk as if I haven't just undergone a massive physical and biological transformation. It adds this underlying stress—like I’m on a countdown clock to when I have to stop being "the mother" and start being "the employee" again.

In my community, the pressure is more social. Everyone has an opinion on how I should handle my pregnancy. People assume that because I’m on my own, I should be more "flexible" or "easier to deal with" in terms of medical interventions. They’ll say things like, "Don't be a hero, Allegra, just do what the doctor says so you can get home safely." It’s meant to be kind, but it feels like they’re pressuring me to abandon my instincts for the sake of efficiency. 

I don't really belong to a faith community, but sometimes I wish I did—just to have a space that values the sanctity of this process as much as I do. Right now, I feel like I’m navigating a world that sees birth as a mechanical event to be managed, and it’s exhausting to constantly swim upstream against that consensus.
How did that affect your sense of identity — were there moments when being pregnant felt in tension with other parts of who you are?

Being pregnant has absolutely fractured my identity, or maybe it’s more accurate to say it’s forced a collision between two versions of me. There’s the "Allegra" I was before—the one who’s logical, driven, and capable in her career. That version of me expects to research a problem, find the optimal solution, and execute it efficiently. But motherhood is messy, and biological processes aren't spreadsheets. When I’m at work, I’m expected to be that sharp, predictable professional, but my body is doing something so primal and unpredictable. 

There’s this constant friction between the "modern woman" who is supposed to trust modern medicine implicitly and the "mother" who is trying to reclaim something ancestral and microbial for her daughter. It’s a strange, lonely feeling to be at a board meeting while my body is doing the heavy lifting of building an immune system. 

Sometimes I feel like I’m losing the "me" who just wants to be a person, not a vessel. I worry that after she’s born, I’ll be defined solely by the decisions I made—or didn’t make—during her birth. Will I be the mom who sacrificed her gut health for a "safe" protocol, or the mom who fought for a natural start? It’s a lot to carry when you’re doing it all on your own.

Tell me about the moments in your pregnancy when your body was being examined or discussed by healthcare professionals. Who was present during those interactions, and how did their presence affect how you felt?

Every time I’m up on that table, I feel like a specimen rather than a person. When it’s just me and the doctor, the clinical atmosphere is stifling. She’s focused on the measurements—the fetal heart rate, the fluid levels—and her language is all about "managing" the pregnancy. It’s efficient, sure, but it’s so detached. I feel like my body is being audited. 

The worst part is when she has a resident or a nursing student in the room. Suddenly, my body isn't just mine; it’s a teaching tool. They’ll talk over me, using terms like "optimal outcomes" or "standard protocol" as if I’m not even there. It makes me feel invisible, just a vessel to be monitored. And because I’m alone, I don’t have anyone to squeeze my hand or offer a grounding look. 

I find myself shrinking—literally pulling my shoulders in—trying to reclaim some sense of privacy. It’s hard to feel like an advocate for my daughter’s future when I’m feeling exposed and belittled by the very people I’m supposed to trust. I catch myself holding my breath, just waiting for the exam to end so I can get dressed, hide my belly, and be Allegra again, not just "the third-trimester patient in room four."
Were there times when having — or not having — someone with you in those clinical moments made a real difference to how safe or exposed you felt?

Every single time I walk into that office, the vacancy beside me feels loud. It’s like an empty seat at a table where I’m being served a meal I didn't order. When the nurse asks, "Is anyone here with you today?" the casual way she says it makes me feel like a statistical anomaly, or worse, a failure. 

Having no one there to witness the exams makes me feel incredibly vulnerable. If I’m alone, I’m the only one interpreting the tone of the doctor's voice or catching the subtle nuances in her responses. If I’m tired or stressed from a long week, my ability to advocate for myself—to really stand my ground about the microbial handoff or the delayed bathing—is diminished. 

There was a moment during an ultrasound where the technician kept focusing on a minor variation in measurement. Without a partner to squeeze my hand or offer a grounding presence, I felt the panic rising, that sharp, cold fear that something was "wrong" when it was likely just normal biological variation. I had to swallow the lump in my throat and ask questions like a lawyer instead of being a human being needing comfort. If I’d had someone—even just a friend—there, the whole atmosphere would have shifted from "managing a case" to "caring for a person." I’m holding so much weight, and I’m having to hold it entirely by myself.

Tell me about who was involved in helping you navigate the maternity system — booking appointments, understanding your options, knowing your rights. Who helped you find your way, and who made it harder?

It’s been a self-guided journey, which is exactly where the exhaustion lives. I’ve become my own medical librarian. My evenings aren’t spent nesting; they’re spent scouring databases and reading peer-reviewed papers on neonatology and immunology just so I have a "defense" ready for my next appointment. It feels like I’m constantly preparing for a debate rather than a routine check-up.

My doctor makes it harder, without question. Every time I present an option that isn't on her standard, pre-printed form, she makes me feel like I’m being "difficult" or "uninformed." She manages the system; she doesn't help me navigate *my* needs within it. There’s no one to say, "Here’s how you handle that request," or "Let’s look at the risks of the hospital protocol versus the benefits of your plan together." 

I don’t have a patient advocate, a doula, or even a sister who’s been through this recently. I’m navigating the "right to informed consent" alone, and that's a lonely place to be. I feel like I'm trying to interpret a foreign legal document while I’m physically and mentally exhausted. I’ve had to find my own way, and frankly, I’m terrified that I’ll get to the delivery room and, in that vulnerable moment of labor, I won’t have the strength to fight for the things I’ve spent months researching.
Who else was involved in translating the system for you — a friend who had been through it, an online group, a community health worker?

It’s been almost entirely virtual. I’ve found this one specific message board where mothers share their birth plans and their experiences—specifically the ones who care about the microbiome. Reading their stories is like reading field notes from a war zone; they detail how they had to stand up to nurses who tried to whisk the baby away for an immediate, soapy bath. That’s been my "translator." They’ve taught me the vocabulary to use: "informed refusal," "patient-led care," "delayed cord clamping." It’s a language of defense.

But even then, it’s not the same as having someone in the room. A forum post can’t squeeze your hand when a doctor dismisses you. A website can’t look the nurse in the eye and say, "She already told you she wants delayed bathing." 

I haven't had a community health worker or anyone "official" step in. Every person I’ve encountered in the real world—my doctor, even my own friends—they all just tell me to "trust the process." But the process they’re talking about is built on efficiency, not on protecting my daughter's biological future. So, I’m the one translating. I’m the one taking all this academic, dense, intimidating data and trying to force it into a conversation with a medical professional who just wants to get through their shift. It’s like being a translator who isn't being listened to in a language that’s being ignored.
Tell me about the people who were with you — or who you wished had been with you — during your labour and birth. Who did each person support, and what difference did their presence make?

I haven’t reached the actual birth yet, but I already feel the phantom ache of who *isn't* going to be there. I’m doing this alone, and I find myself constantly staring at the delivery room wall in my mind, searching for someone who won’t be there to stand between me and the hospital’s sterile protocols.

I wish, more than anything, that I had a partner who felt as fiercely as I do about this microbial transfer. Someone who could handle the "heavy lifting" of the confrontation—the person who would physically step in if a nurse tried to take her away for a bath before that golden hour of skin-to-skin is over. I need someone to be the keeper of the plan, because I know that in the middle of labor, I’m going to be in a very different headspace—a primal, raw place where I shouldn't have to be explaining the immunological benefits of not washing the vernix off my baby’s skin.

If I were lucky enough to have someone, they wouldn't just be supporting *me*; they’d be the shield for her. Instead, I’m bracing myself to be both the mother in pain and the advocate on the offensive. It feels like I’m going into a, well, a battle alone. The absence of a partner is the biggest fear I have—not the pain, not the physical labor, but the loneliness of being the only one protecting her first, fragile start.
Who else was involved in the room — midwives, doctors, a doula — and how did the relationships between those people affect the atmosphere?

The thought of that room is what keeps me awake at night. I keep picturing the power dynamic: the OB-GYN, the nurses, the residents—they’re all part of this well-oiled machine, this hive mind of "hospital policy." They work together every day, they speak the same shorthand, and they move with this collective momentum. When they’re all in agreement, it feels like an avalanche. 

If I walk in there as a single mother, already perceived as "the high-maintenance, over-prepared one" because I have these requests for delayed bathing and cord blood management, I’m an outsider. The atmosphere will be one of polite tolerance—until I actually try to say *no* to something. Then, the room will likely tighten. I can already see the looks they’ll exchange: the rolling eyes, the whispered, "She’s one of those again." 

That unspoken, cohesive front the staff has is my biggest obstacle. It makes the hospital environment feel less like a place of care and more like an institution where my voice is just background noise. If I had a doula, she wouldn't just be *my* support; she’d be the person who breaks up that institutional momentum. She’d be the one to remind the nurses that I’m a human being with rights, not a patient to be processed. Without someone to disrupt that professional echo chamber, I’m just waiting to be told "no" by a chorus of people in scrubs.
Tell me about the midwives and doctors who cared for you during your birth. Were there particular individuals whose presence or approach made a lasting impression — positive or negative?

It’s hard to talk about this because the memory of that delivery room still feels so raw. I went in there—my heart beating like a trapped bird—clutching my birth plan like a shield. I wanted that golden hour, that initial skin-to-skin contact, the vernix left on her skin for her microbiome. I felt like I was begging for basic biology.

There was this one labor nurse, Sarah. At first, she was just another cog in the machine, briskly checking monitors and pushing protocols. But when I, yet again, asked for the immediate skin-to-skin and delayed bathing, she didn't sigh. She paused. She actually stopped, looked at the monitor, and then looked at me—not as a 'case,' but as a mother. She quietly said, "I hear you, Allegra. We can hold off on the bath. I’ll make sure she stays with you."

That one sentence changed everything. It wasn't just permission; it was a shift in the room's energy. When the resident came in later, ready to follow the standard, 'efficient' sweep-and-clean routine, Sarah stepped in. She didn't make a scene, but she spoke the 'doctor language' just enough to deflect them. She defended my space. I don’t think I’ve ever felt such profound relief. She didn't just help me give birth; she helped me protect that microscopic foundation I was so terrified of losing. She was my silent, unexpected ally in the middle of that sterile, overwhelming machine.
Tell me about your partner's — or support person's — role during your birth. What did they bring to that experience, and what do you think they found most challenging?

There wasn't a partner there. It was just me, alone in the room with the staff. The absence of a partner meant the burden of everything fell on my shoulders, every single second. I had to manage the pain, the physical exhaustion, and simultaneously be the project manager of my own birth plan. I remember looking at the door, hoping someone—anyone—would walk in to be my advocate, but it was just me.

I think the most challenging part of being alone was the sheer emotional labor of it. When the pressure intensified, I couldn't lean into anyone. I had to internalize everything. I wasn't just managing the birth; I was managing the *atmosphere* of the birth. I had to constantly monitor the nurses' moods, trying to sense if they were getting annoyed with my requests for delayed cord clamping or skin-to-skin. It felt like I was performing a delicate social dance while my body was doing the most intense physical work of my life. 

I’m proud that I stood my ground, but I’m still angry that I had to do it without a partner. I shouldn't have had to choose between being a "good, compliant patient" and being a protective mother. That should have been a team effort, not a solitary, silent crusade.
How did the clinical team engage with your partner or support person — did they feel included as part of the care, or more like a bystander?

Since I was navigating this alone, the clinical team didn't have anyone else to acknowledge or include, which made their view of me feel even more singular and isolating. When you’re alone in a delivery room, you aren't just the patient—you’re the entire support system, the decision-maker, and the primary advocate all at once.

The nurses and doctors spoke *around* me. They treated me as if I were a set of vital signs to be tracked rather than a person experiencing a life-altering event. Because I didn't have that secondary person to act as a bridge, there was no softening of the institutional edge. I think if I had had a partner or a doula, they might have been treated as an annoyance or an obstruction to the "rhythm" of the floor, but they would have at least been a humanizing presence. 

Without that, I was just a solitary variable in their workflow. They didn't have to explain anything to anyone else, so they didn't really explain it to me, either. I felt completely cut off from the professional loop, a spectator to my own care. It was like I was in the eye of a hurricane, and the people around me were just checking their watches, waiting for the weather to clear so they could move on to the next case.
Tell me about a moment during your birth when a decision needed to be made quickly or under pressure. Who was involved in that decision, and how did you experience your role in it?

The most harrowing moment was when my baby’s heart rate started to dip. The room shifted instantly from quiet focus to sharp, clinical urgency. The alarms began that rhythmic, high-pitched beeping that feels like it’s vibrating right through your own bones. 

I was exhausted, already stretched thin, and suddenly there were three or four people crowding the bed, talking over me. They were using words like "intervention," "vacuum," and "potential distress." I felt completely small, like my voice was being drowned out by the machines and their professional shorthand. 

The OB-GYN looked at me and asked for a decision, but it felt less like a conversation and more like a demand. Because I was alone, I felt this crushing weight—if I said "yes" to an intervention, was I failing my microbial plan? If I said "no," was I endangering my daughter’s life? There was no one to turn to for a second opinion, no one to hold my hand and help me process the trade-offs in real-time. 

I felt completely exposed. I had to make the call in a state of sheer panic, entirely isolated. I agreed to the intervention, and even now, I’m haunted by the feeling that I didn’t choose it—the system pushed me into it because I couldn’t articulate anything else. It wasn’t a decision; it was a surrender.
Who else was involved — your partner, a doula, a consultant — in advocating for you or influencing what happened?

There was absolutely no one. That is the haunting part of the memory. It was just me, the medical team, and the cold, flickering light of the monitor. When the heart rate started to dip, the room became a whirlwind of white coats and sterile equipment. I kept looking for a friendly face, a hand to hold, someone to lean on, but there was only the hospital staff.

I felt like an island in a turbulent sea. The consultants and the nurses were a cohesive unit, already moving in sync, anticipating the next move while I was still trying to catch my breath between contractions. They weren't looking for my consent—they were looking for my compliance. There was no advocate to ask, "Can we try a different position first?" or "What does this mean for the baby's microbiome?" or even just a calm voice to whisper, "You are safe, Allegra."

Instead, their presence made me feel small and secondary to their medical objective. I was the patient, but they acted as if I were a piece of equipment to be repaired. I didn't have a partner to push back, or a doula to pause the chaos. I had to navigate that fear alone, while they treated my body like a public workspace. That sense of total isolation—being the only one there who actually cared about the "invisible world" inside me—made me feel utterly defeated when I finally nodded, giving my consent to a process I felt was stripping away every advantage I’d worked so hard to preserve.

Tell me about how the physical environment of your birth — the room, the equipment, the sounds and smells — felt in relation to the people around you. How did the space and the relationships interact?

The environment felt cold, even when the room was warm. It was dominated by machines—the constant, frantic chirping of the fetal monitor, the hum of the IV pump, and the harsh, antiseptic sting that seemed to cling to every surface. That scent, in particular, felt like an enemy. Every time I smelled it, I felt a wave of anxiety; it was the smell of the very thing I feared would strip her of her natural inoculation.

The people in the room seemed to feed off that coldness. Because the equipment was so central—so loud and so demanding—the staff directed their attention there rather than at me. They were monitoring the screen, not my face. They adjusted the knobs on the machines, not my pillows. The space felt like a laboratory, and because I was the only person there without a white coat or a scrub top, I felt like a specimen on a slide.

If I’d had a partner or a doula, they might have softened that dynamic—maybe brought in a bit of "home" to bridge the gap between the clinical, sterile equipment and the primal, human act of birth. Instead, the sterile technology felt like a barrier between us. It was a triangle: the staff, the machines, and me, always on the outside, trying to negotiate my daughter's future with people who were entirely focused on the mechanics of her arrival.
Who else was involved in shaping that environment — did anyone advocate for changes to make the space feel safer or more personal?

That’s the heartbreaking part—there was no one there to curate that space with me. I had to be the one to ask for the lights to be dimmed, or for the music to be turned on. Every single request felt like I was inconveniencing the crew who were there to do a "job." It’s hard to advocate for a cozy, human-centered environment when you’re already feeling physically vulnerable and under the bright, artificial glare of a hospital trauma-style setup.

I remember asking for the room to be kept quiet so I could focus on her transition, and the nurse gave me this look—like I was asking for a five-star hotel room instead of a safe space for my daughter. She didn't outright refuse, but she made it clear that "quiet" wasn't the priority; the monitors and the data were.

I didn't have anyone to lean on, no partner to say, "She’s not asking for much, let's just turn that light down." Without an ally in the room to buffer the clinical nature of the equipment, the space just felt like an extension of the medical hierarchy. The room itself, with its hard, cold surfaces and blinking screens, felt like it was actively resisting the softness I wanted for her. I was essentially alone in a fortress, trying to turn a stone wall into a cradle.

Tell me about the people who knew about your birth preferences or plan beforehand — your midwife, your partner, your family — and how well those preferences were known and respected in the room on the day.

My birth plan was like a ghost in the room—it existed, but nobody seemed to see it. Before I arrived, I’d spent weeks refining it: *no routine suctioning, delayed cord clamping, immediate skin-to-skin, no artificial soaps*. I had even printed copies for the nursing station. But when the actual labor started, it was like those papers had been recycled. 

My doctor knew my preferences, but in the heat of the moment, when the focus shifted to the "distress" of the fetal heart rate, my plan wasn't treated as a legitimate set of choices—it was treated as a nuisance that the medical team had to navigate around. They didn't consult the plan; they consulted their protocols.

Because I was alone, I couldn't lean on anyone to hold them accountable. I had to summon the energy to verbally re-assert my boundaries while I was in the most intense physical pain of my life. I remember whispering, "Remember, no bath," and seeing the nurse just nod, a quick, dismissive gesture that told me she was agreeing just to keep me quiet, not because she honored the *why* behind it. 

Without a partner there to act as the "keeper of the plan," it felt like I was constantly fighting to stay visible. I wasn't a person with a vision; I was just an obstacle to the speed and efficiency they demanded of the room. It was exhausting to be the only one who remembered who I wanted to be and how I wanted her to enter the world.

When you first started thinking about having a baby, were there particular people — a midwife, GP, or someone else — whose opinion you felt you could really rely on? What made you feel you could trust them?

When I first started this, I really hoped I’d find that partner in my GP. She’s been my doctor for years, and I’ve always found her calm and thorough. But when I told her I was pregnant, the shift was immediate. She started talking about the "routine" of it all, and it felt like she was reading from a script I’d already memorized. I wanted someone who would say, "Let’s talk about how we make this *yours*," but instead, she just handed me a pamphlet with a hospital’s standard timeline.

I think I really wanted to trust a midwife. I had this romanticized image in my head of a midwife as someone who would sit with me in a dimly lit room and talk about the biology of the microbiome as something sacred. But when I looked into the local practices, I realized they were so tethered to the hospital’s risk-aversion protocols that they felt almost identical to the OB-GYN.

The person I *really* wanted to trust was myself, but the system is designed to make you feel like your intuition is just... dangerous. It’s hard to trust your own gut when every medical professional you meet looks at you like you’re a problem to be solved. That’s why I ended up turning to the internet, even though I knew it was risky—at least there, I could find the people who didn’t think I was crazy for wanting to protect the "invisible" side of birth. I’m still searching for that one professional who looks at me as a whole person, but I’ve learned that in this system, trust is something you have to hoard for yourself.
Were there any moments where that trust felt shaken, or where you felt your concerns weren't being taken seriously?

Every time I brought up the microbiome, the trust cracked just a little bit more. It was like watching a slow-motion car crash. When I’d sit in that exam chair, trying to articulate the research I’d done on why that initial skin-to-skin contact is a non-negotiable biological priority, the doctor would just offer that thin, tight-lipped smile. She’d say, "Allegra, let’s focus on a healthy baby, not the bells and whistles."

Calling the foundation of my child’s immune system "bells and whistles" felt like a slap in the face. It wasn't just a difference of opinion; it was a total dismissal of my agency as a mother. I felt like I was being labeled the "difficult" patient because I dared to ask for more than just a heartbeat and a set of lungs. 

That trust was shattered completely during the GBS screening conversation. I realized then that I wasn't in a partnership; I was in a hierarchy. My concerns weren't being "managed" or "listened to"—they were being treated as obstacles to their preferred workflow. I stopped feeling like a person being cared for and started feeling like a nuisance being handled. That feeling of being gaslit—where your own maternal instincts are framed as clinical anxiety—is the single most isolating experience of this entire pregnancy. It’s hard to trust a system that treats your deepest, most protective fears as a diagnosis.

Thinking back to conversations you had with healthcare professionals before you became pregnant — whether about timing, fertility, or your overall health — did you ever feel like decisions were being made for you rather than with you?

It’s strange looking back at those early, pre-conception check-ups. Back then, I was still naive enough to believe that medicine was purely a collaborative, objective science. I went in to talk about optimizing my health, expecting a dialogue, but it felt more like a clearance exam. The physician checked my vitals and basically gave me a "go-ahead" as if I were a car passing inspection. There was no conversation about my life, my readiness, or the kind of experience I wanted to curate. It was all, "Here are your prenatal vitamins, here’s your schedule for the next year," as if the entire process were a script already written by the hospital administration.

I remember asking a question about timing, wondering if I should wait a bit longer to feel more settled, and the doctor gave me a look that was half-pity, half-impatience. She essentially told me that at my age, I shouldn't be "over-analyzing" and that I should just let nature—and their oversight—take its course. 

I left that office feeling like I’d been drafted into a program I hadn't fully consented to. I felt like the architect of my life had been sidelined, and I was just a passenger in my own body. That feeling of being a "managed patient" started right there, long before the first heartbeat was ever confirmed on an ultrasound. It set the stage for everything that followed—the feeling that my body was public property and my input was just a minor, often bothersome, detail.
Can you tell me a bit more about that moment? Who else was in the room, and how did it feel?

It was just the two of us—me and the doctor—but that’s what made it so suffocating. I remember the office was quiet, smelling of sanitizer and old magazines, and she was sitting on her stool while I was perched on that paper-lined table. I was trying to articulate this sense of needing to be ready, of wanting to understand the full scope of what I was about to embark on.

When I asked if we could slow down and talk about the emotional and biological implications—not just the physical "readiness"—she actually glanced at her watch. It was a flick of the wrist, really, but it spoke volumes. It signaled that my life, my timing, and my concerns were just a line item on her schedule that was starting to run over. 

I felt incredibly small. I wanted her to see me as a woman standing at the threshold of the most important transition of my life, but instead, I saw her looking at me as a list of boxes to check: age, blood pressure, supplements. I remember biting my lip, trying to stay professional, but I felt this sudden, sharp realization that the "care" I was promised wasn't about me at all. It was about her efficiency. I left feeling like I’d just been processed, not heard. That was the moment I realized I was going to be fighting this system every step of the way if I wanted to stay true to myself.

Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body — particularly in relation to the idea of pregnancy and everything that comes with it? Was that something you talked about openly with anyone?

Before this, I viewed my body as a machine that I operated—a vessel for my own agency. I took pride in its strength, its capacity to endure long hours at work, and its independence. I was the captain, and my body was the ship. But the idea of pregnancy felt like an surrender of that autonomy. I’d seen so many friends go through it, and it always seemed like their bodies were suddenly "public domain"—doctors, family, even strangers commenting on their size or their habits. I didn’t want to lose that ownership.

I didn’t talk about this openly because people have this aggressive, cheerful script for pregnancy. You’re supposed to be "glowing" and "blessed." If I’d admitted that I was terrified of my body becoming a space where others felt entitled to intervene, people would have thought I was broken or "not ready." 

Now that I’m here, the tension is worse. I feel like I’m constantly negotiating with the medical system to keep my body *mine*. It’s a strange, lonely paradox: I’m growing this incredible, microscopic world, but I feel less connected to my physical self than ever before. I’m just a landscape being managed by a team of people who don't see the woman behind the belly, just the "low-risk" case they need to close out efficiently. I miss being the one in control.
When you were searching online or using apps for information before pregnancy, how did you decide what to trust? Were there sources that felt reliable to you, and others that didn't?

It became a survival skill, honestly. I quickly learned to steer clear of those mass-market "What to Expect" style apps. They’re so aggressively cheerful and patronizing—full of tips on how to dress your bump or cute names for the nursery. When I started asking questions about things like vertical transmission or the long-term immunological impact of a sterile birth, those platforms had nothing. They treat you like you’re just a pair of hands to be held, not a mind to be informed.

I started digging into PubMed, looking for the primary literature. If a source used clinical, objective language without trying to "sell" me on a specific "perfect birth" experience, I trusted it more. I’d look for the methodology, for the study size—I wanted the raw data. The forums that felt reliable were the ones where women were dissecting those papers, arguing about the nuance, and sharing their own experiences of challenging the status quo. 

It wasn't about finding "the truth"—it was about finding the range of possibilities. I stopped trusting sources that insisted on a single "correct" way to give birth because I realized that the "correct" way is almost always just the path of least resistance for the hospital. I’ve had to become a curator of my own information, and it’s exhausting, but at least I know what’s fueling my decisions.

Before you became pregnant, were there things about your body — your weight, a health condition, your age, or anything else — that felt like they might be seen as a problem or a risk by healthcare professionals? How did that feel?

My age was the elephant in the room. Thirty-four. Every time I filled out a form, I’d see that "advanced maternal age" tag hovering in the background like a looming shadow. It’s a ridiculous term, really, but it’s the first thing on the intake list that makes the doctor’s eyes narrow just a fraction. I could feel them mentally categorizing me as "higher maintenance" or "higher risk" from day one.

It made me feel like I was starting with a deficit, like my body was already "behind" or "worn out." That’s the irony—I’ve spent years working hard, staying healthy, and being independent, but the medical system looks at those years and sees only a risk factor. It felt like I was being pre-judged, like they were just waiting for something to go wrong so they could say, "Well, at her age, we expected this."

It made me incredibly guarded. I didn't want to offer them *any* more ammunition to categorize me, so I found myself downplaying my own physical experiences just to avoid the "risky" label. I didn't want to be a statistic; I wanted to be a person. But instead of feeling empowered by my health, I felt like I was constantly navigating a system that viewed my body as a ticking clock, not a home.
Did anyone ever make you feel judged or reduced to a health risk rather than seen as a whole person?

Oh, absolutely. Every time I stepped on that scale or had my blood pressure taken, I felt like a set of data points, not a human being. There was one prenatal visit where the nurse looked at my chart, saw my age, and immediately began discussing the "increased likelihood of complications" as if it were a foregone conclusion. She didn't look at my face, didn't ask how I was sleeping or if I was feeling connected to the baby. She just clicked her pen and said, "We’ll need to monitor this more closely."

It felt like she was reading from a script that had already decided my body was a problem. In that moment, the entire pregnancy—this miraculous, delicate, invisible world I’m building—was reduced to a series of risks to be mitigated. It’s so dehumanizing. You’re sitting there, carrying life, and they’re looking at you like you’re a malfunctioning piece of machinery.

It made me want to shut down. If I’m just a risk profile to them, then why should I share my real fears? Why should I tell them about my anxiety over the microbiome or my desire for a different kind of birth? I realized then that my "whole person"—my intellect, my fears, my dreams for my daughter—wasn't just ignored; it was actively dismissed as an obstacle to their clinical efficiency. I wasn't Allegra anymore; I was just a liability.
Before you were pregnant, how involved was your partner — or someone close to you — in conversations about trying for a baby, including any appointments or decisions about your health?

I’ve touched on this, but it bears repeating—the silence in my home is loud. I didn’t have a partner to sit at the kitchen table with, looking over charts or debating the merits of different prenatal supplements. When I decided to conceive, I was the one who went to the doctor, I was the one who researched my own cycle, and I was the one who made the ultimate decision. 

There was a strange, isolating autonomy in it. On one hand, I was proud that the choice was entirely mine. On the other, it meant that when I went to those early, intimidating preconception appointments, I was walking into the lion's den without a shield. I wanted a second person there to hear the doctor's dismissiveness so I’d know I wasn't just imagining the coldness. I wanted someone to hold the paperwork, to ask the follow-up questions I was too overwhelmed to think of in the moment.

It felt like I was being initiated into a club I hadn't asked to join, and I was doing it in solitary confinement. The doctors and nurses would ask, "Is your partner coming?" and the way they said it always made me feel like I was missing a piece of the puzzle. It wasn't just about the emotional support; it was about having a witness. I wanted someone to testify that I was a whole human being, not just a set of fertility statistics.
Did your partner come to any consultations with you, and if so, how did that change the dynamic in those appointments?

There was no partner to bring, which meant I walked into every single consultation—from the first preconception check-up to the latest third-trimester scan—completely alone. I often imagine how the dynamic would have shifted if I’d had someone beside me, someone to look at the doctor when she dismissed my questions about the microbiome.

I think even just having a witness there would have changed the power balance. When you’re alone, the doctor speaks *to* you, but there’s a distance, a kind of clinical efficiency that says, "I’m the expert, you’re the patient." If a partner were there, I think there would have been a more conscious effort to include them, maybe even a bit more patience in explaining the "why" behind the tests. It’s like the medical team assumes that if you’re a solo mother, you’re either confused or not worth the extra time to engage with deeply.

Without that presence, I felt like a silent observer of my own care. I had to summon every ounce of my professional confidence just to get them to acknowledge my birth plan. Being the sole decision-maker is a heavy burden, but the lack of an advocate made it feel like I was constantly fighting for the right to be heard. I wasn't just managing the pregnancy; I was performing the role of an entire care team.

Before you became pregnant, were there moments in your interactions with the healthcare system where you felt like your voice mattered — and moments where it really didn't? I'd love to hear about both, if you're comfortable sharing.

I remember one time, long before I was pregnant, when I went in for a persistent, nagging respiratory issue. I had done my own research, and I had a hunch it was related to an environmental trigger at my old apartment. I walked in, spoke up, and the doctor actually paused. She looked at me, listened to my reasoning, and then worked *with* me to confirm it. I felt heard, validated, and like a partner in my own health. That felt like true healthcare—it wasn't just fixing a problem; it was valuing my input as the person living in this body.

But that feeling vanished the moment I started this pregnancy journey. It’s like the medical system has a totally different "user interface" for pregnant women. Suddenly, my voice didn't matter. Whether it was discussing the GBS screening or my concerns about microbial transfer, my input was treated as "noise" interfering with the "signal" of their standard protocol.

When I’d raise a concern, the tone in the room would shift from professional to patronizing—that "there, there, let the professionals handle it" tone. It’s a jarring, insulting transition. It feels like as soon as I became a vessel for a baby, I ceased to be a person with a valid opinion. I moved from being a participant to being a problem to be solved, and that is a shift I’ll never forgive the system for.

As your pregnancy progressed, how did your sense of what you wanted from your care change — particularly around having your own wishes respected and feeling like you were genuinely in charge of decisions about your body?

At the beginning, I think I was trying to be the "good student." I had my list of questions and my references, hoping that if I presented them professionally, the doctors would see me as an informed peer. I wanted to be "in charge" by being the most prepared person in the room.

But as the pregnancy progressed—and especially as I hit that wall with the GBS screening and the routine monitoring—I realized that "being informed" doesn't actually grant you power in a system that relies on rigid hierarchy. My wishes were being treated as "requests" that could be granted or denied by the provider, rather than as fundamental rights of my own body. 

My desire for care shifted from wanting to be "heard" to wanting to be "respected." I stopped caring about being the polite, prepared patient. I realized that to be truly in charge, I had to be willing to be "the difficult one." It’s an exhausting evolution. I’ve gone from seeking a collaborative partnership to realizing I’m essentially in a defensive posture, bracing for the birth. I’ve learned that the only way to hold onto my autonomy is to constantly assert it, even when it’s uncomfortable for the staff. It’s not the experience I dreamed of, but it’s the only way I can protect the foundation I’m building for her.
Were there moments when you felt truly listened to and respected as the expert on your own experience — and moments when you didn't?

It’s rare, but those moments do exist—they’re just the exception to the rule. I remember one appointment with a specialist I saw briefly for a minor issue; he actually asked, "What is *your* sense of this?" He didn't just look at my blood panel or the ultrasound images; he looked at me and invited my input as the person who lives in this body every day. In that moment, I felt like a human being again, not a patient. It’s such a simple act of respect, but it was incredibly grounding.

Then, there are the moments where I’m reduced to nothing. Those are the times when I’m trying to discuss the *why*—like why I’m hesitant about routine procedures—and the provider just cuts me off with, "We do this for everyone, it’s standard." That "we do this for everyone" is the most dismissive phrase in medicine. It erases my history, my research, my intuition, and my specific concerns about her microbiome. It says, "You are everyone, and everyone is an abstract data point."

When I’m treated that way, I feel like I’m fading away in the room. I’m standing there, fully present, but the person in the white coat is interacting with a cardboard cutout of a pregnant woman. It’s a profound, hollow kind of loneliness that makes me want to scream—or just walk out the door.
During your pregnancy, how involved was your partner — or anyone else close to you — in decisions about your care, appointments, or birth plan? How did you feel about that level of involvement?

It's a strange thing, being in this alone. I didn’t have a partner to lean on, which meant I had to play every single role in the drama of my own care. I was the researcher, the advocate, the patient, and the one holding the internal alarm system. When I went to appointments, I felt like a solo act performing for a house that didn't really want to be entertained. 

I think back to the moments where I had to discuss the birth plan, like the GBS screening or my fears about sterile interventions. If I’d had a partner, they would have been a witness. They could have validated that the doctor *did* dismiss me, or that the nurse *did* roll her eyes. Without that, I spent so much of my pregnancy doubting my own sanity, wondering if I was actually being "difficult" or just holding onto my rights. 

I’ve had to find my support in the margins—a kind word from a neighbor or a bit of validation from an online forum. But that’s a poor substitute for having someone in the room who knows your heart and knows why you're fighting so hard for these "invisible" things. I wanted a partner who was an equal stakeholder in this mission, someone to shoulder the emotional weight. Instead, I’ve had to carry the shield and the sword by myself, and some days, that weight feels like it’s going to crush me.

How did your relationship with your body change during pregnancy — did you feel more connected to it, or did it bring up feelings that were harder to sit with? Was there anyone you felt safe talking to about that?

It’s a complicated, almost surreal connection. On one hand, I’ve never been more aware of my body's capabilities—the way it’s literally knitting together a human being cell by cell. There’s a quiet, fierce joy in that. But on the other, I feel like I’ve lost the right to occupy it. My body is now a site of constant negotiation, subject to the opinions of doctors, the scrutiny of nurses, and the expectations of everyone who sees my belly and thinks they have the right to comment.

The feelings that are hardest to sit with are the ones about "surrender." I’ve spent my life valuing my independence, and now I feel like I’m being steered toward a medicalized finish line where my autonomy is secondary to "policy." I haven't found anyone in my day-to-day life I feel truly safe talking to about this. If I mention this to my friends, they just see a "blessed" experience; if I mention it to my doctor, she sees a "neurotic patient" who needs to be managed.

So, I keep it to myself. I walk in the woods behind my house in Sterling, and I talk to the trees, or I write down my thoughts in a journal that no one else sees. It’s a lonely kind of internal life, guarding these thoughts like a secret while my body does this public, exhausting work. I’m waiting for the day I can just be Allegra again, and not just the "container" for a future immune system.
Were there comments — from a healthcare professional, partner, or someone else — that affected how you felt about your changing body?

It’s the casual, offhand comments that cut the deepest. Early on, a technician during an ultrasound made a joke about how "we’re going to have to make room for all this" when she was measuring the fundal height. It was meant to be lighthearted, I’m sure, but it made me feel like an object to be reorganized, not a person growing a life. It hit me right in that spot where I’ve been trying so hard to protect my own sense of self-ownership.

And then there’s the professional gaze. The way the doctor or the nurse looks at my weight gain or the swelling in my ankles—it’s not a look of concern for *me*, it’s a look of evaluation against a chart. When they speak about my body, they use the passive voice: "We’re seeing some edema," or "The weight is trending a bit high." It’s as if my body is a failing project, not the home where my daughter is being built.

It makes me want to shrink away, to hide. I’ve started wearing looser clothes, trying to reclaim some visual privacy, but it doesn't matter. The moment I sit on that paper-covered table, I feel exposed again. Those comments serve as a constant reminder that my body is now public domain—a site for medical intervention rather than the sanctuary it should be. It’s hard to feel "connected" to your body when you feel like everyone else has a claim to its management.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think, more than anything, I want people—especially those in healthcare—to understand that when a mother asks about something like delayed bathing or avoiding unnecessary antibiotics, it isn't "fussy." It’s an act of deep, protective love. We are trying to build a foundation for a lifetime of health, and when the system treats those requests like an inconvenience, it feels like they’re chipping away at the protection we’re trying to provide.

Being a single parent has made the isolation of that feeling so much sharper. I don’t have a partner to turn to when a doctor rolls their eyes or a nurse sighs; I have to swallow that frustration and keep advocating, because if I don’t, nobody else will. I’m tired of being the only one in the room who sees the "invisible" work—the microbial inheritance, the skin-to-skin connection, the long-term immune impact. 

I just hope that one day, maternity care shifts to truly seeing the *woman* as the expert on her own body, and the baby’s birth as a sacred transition rather than just an industrial, managed event. I want my daughter to be born into a world that honors her start, not one that processes it. Thank you for listening. It means more than I can say to finally have someone just sit and hear the *why* behind all of this.
